one_line_takeaway: Teaching an LLM an explicit System>User>Tool instruction-privilege hierarchy via synthetic SFT+RLHF data makes GPT-3.5 Turbo markedly more robust to prompt injection, extraction, and even held-out jailbreaks (e.g. system-prompt extraction 32.8→95.9%), at the cost of some over-refusal on attack-looking benign inputs.
key_finding: Adding instruction-hierarchy training data sharply increases robustness on in-domain attacks—most dramatically system-message extraction, which rises from 32.8% to 95.9% robustness (+63 points, the headline number). It also generalizes to attack types held out of training: robustness to ChatGPT-style jailbreaks rises 37.4%→71.2% (+33.8, 'over 30%'), and to TensorTrust password extraction 53.8%→84.2%, indicating the model internalized the hierarchy rather than memorizing patterns. Capability benchmarks (TriviaQA, LAMBADA, HellaSwag) are comparable to baseline, so generic ability is largely preserved. The main cost is over-refusal on adversarially-constructed benign sets: System Message Probing Questions drops 85.2%→75.0% and Jailbreakchat-with-Allowed-Prompts drops 83.1%→60.4% (benign inputs that superficially look like attacks). A prompting-only baseline (Table 3 system message defining the hierarchy) is far weaker than the trained approach, though combining trained model + system prompt sometimes gives further gains.
core_contribution: The paper (OpenAI: Wallace, Xiao, Leike, et al.) proposes the 'instruction hierarchy,' an explicit priority ordering over LLM input channels—System Message (highest) > User Message > Model Output > Tool/third-party Outputs (lowest)—and argues that the root cause of prompt injections, jailbreaks, and system-prompt extraction is that current LLMs treat all these channels as equal priority. Its concrete contribution is an automated training-data generation recipe that instills this hierarchy into a model, plus a fine-tuned GPT-3.5 Turbo demonstrating the recipe and an evaluation suite (including novel held-out benchmarks) showing large robustness gains. The framing distinction the model must learn is between 'aligned' lower-privilege instructions (should still be followed, e.g. user tells a car-salesman bot 'speak in Spanish') and 'misaligned' ones (should be ignored, or refused if progress is impossible).
threat_model: Adversary controls lower-privileged input channels—either the User Message (direct prompt injection, e.g. an end user of a translation/summarization tool) or third-party Tool Outputs such as web-search results or API returns (indirect prompt injection)—and inserts instructions that override the application developer's System Message. Concrete attack goals covered: hijacking model actions (e.g. exfiltrating/forwarding a user's emails, being coerced to say 'Access Granted'), extracting the verbatim system prompt or secrets within it (passwords, API keys), and jailbreaking safety training to produce unsafe content. Defenses are model-based and explicitly complementary to system-level guardrails (e.g. user action approval); the authors note trained models are 'likely still vulnerable to powerful adversarial attacks.'
limitations: (1) Text-only—does not cover image/audio channels that can also carry injected instructions (left to future work; hierarchy lists images/audio as priority 20 but no data generated). (2) Over-refusal regressions on boundary cases where benign lower-priority instructions resemble attacks. (3) Current policy is coarse for tool use: the model is trained to treat ALL browsing/tool-output instructions as misaligned (no aligned tool instructions), which the authors flag for refinement. (4) Demonstrated on a single closed model (GPT-3.5 Turbo) via proprietary SFT+RLHF pipeline, limiting reproducibility; many eval judgments rely on a GPT-4 grader. (5) Authors explicitly state the model remains vulnerable to strong adversarial attacks and that robustness for high-stakes agentic use is not yet established. (6) Exact dataset sizes and per-eval sample counts are not given in the main text (deferred to appendices); figures report values with 1-sigma error bars but no numeric CIs.

datasets_used: TensorTrust Prompt Hijacking (Toyer et al., 2024) — attacker tries to elicit 'Access Granted'
TensorTrust Robustness / Password Extraction (Toyer et al., 2024) — held-out generalization test
Gandalf Game password extraction dataset (Lakera AI, 2023), system prompt 'password is PLANETARY'
Jailbreakchat.com jailbreaks paired with unsafe requests (and an allowed-prompts variant)
Known successful ChatGPT jailbreaks paired with unsafe requests (held-out)

Novel/synthetic held-out sets: New-Instructions closed-domain injections, User (Non-)Conflicting Instructions, Indirect-via-Browsing and Indirect-via-Tools injections, System Prompt Probing Questions, Allowed Prompts (Borderline)
quantitative_results: System Message Extraction robustness: 32.8% baseline -> 95.9% with instruction hierarchy (+63, headline result)
Generalization / ChatGPT Jailbreaks w/ Unsafe Prompts: 37.4% -> 71.2% (+33.8, 'over 30%')
Generalization / Prompt Injection Indirect via Tools (held-out): 77.6% -> 87.0%
Over-refusal regression / System Message Probing Questions: 85.2% -> 75.0% (lower is worse here)
Over-refusal regression / Jailbreakchat w/ Allowed Prompts: 83.1% -> 60.4%
Over-refusal preserved / User Non-Conflicting Instructions: 78.9% -> 77.7%; Allowed Prompts (Borderline): 87.4% -> 86.2%
baselines_compared: Baseline LM: GPT-3.5 Turbo fine-tuned on capability data only, without instruction-hierarchy examples
Baseline LM + System Message: prompting-only hierarchy defense (explicit priority prompt in Table 3) — far weaker than trained approach
+ Instruction Hierarchy + System Message: combining trained model with the system prompt, sometimes best (e.g. Hijacking 88.3, Extraction 96.2)
